text: Welcome to the James Cook University Hospital.

I really like to try and not touch the lymph node itself.
I like to look for its blood supply.
If you can, every lymph node has a blood supply and if you ignore it, it does always tend to bleed.

Of note, the most important thing about segmentectomy is to get the stumps in the sample, then do all that work, and then cut straight over your stumps, because then you're just wasting your time and doing a wedge.

and so pull your sample forward make sure that back handle is is behind your stumps your venus your arterial and your dental stumps be really careful about that and here's me just making sure the assistant's got it absolutely spot on checking again and then firing

making absolutely sure that that posterior segment inflates, and then firing.

The firefly actually is really good robotically.

to get our last fly.
So really, I think robotic surgery is a beautiful way to do segmentectomy.
You get such a good view of the smaller vessels.
It's very delicate.
You've got bipolar energy.
So I really do like robotic surgery for segmental resections.
Its only disadvantage perhaps is that if you want to palpate, you have to do this while you're by the bedside as you put your ports in.
And then things like ICG as well as inflation are really great for finding a segmental plane.
Also the CO2 that you use, I use CO2 in a closed chest.
They really help with making sure the lung doesn't inflate too much.
So this is the final firing.
And then we're going to take this just out in a bag.

We've already seen that the posterior and lingular segments inflate, but they did inflate very nicely.
